abstract: On-policy reinforcement learning (RL) algorithms are widely used for their strong asymptotic performance and training stability, but they struggle to scale with larger batch sizes, as additional parallel environments yield redundant data due to limited policy-induced diversity. In contrast, Evolutionary Algorithms (EAs) scale naturally and encourage exploration via randomized population-based search, but are often sample-inefficient. We propose Evolutionary Policy Optimization (EPO), a hybrid algorithm that combines the scalability and diversity of EAs with the performance and stability of policy gradients. EPO maintains a population of agents conditioned on latent variables, shares actor-critic network parameters for coherence and memory efficiency, and aggregates diverse experiences into a master agent. Across tasks in dexterous manipulation, legged locomotion, and classic control, EPO outperforms state-of-the-art baselines in sample efficiency, asymptotic performance, and scalability.
title: Evolutionary Policy Optimization

abstract:
Model-free deep reinforcement learning (RL) algorithms have been demonstrated
on a range of challenging decision making and control tasks. However, these
methods typically suffer from two major challenges: very high sample complexity
and brittle convergence properties, which necessitate meticulous hyperparameter
tuning. Both of these challenges severely limit the applicability of such
methods to complex, real-world domains. In this paper, we propose soft
actor-critic, an off-policy actor-critic deep RL algorithm based on the maximum
entropy reinforcement learning framework. In this framework, the actor aims to
maximize expected reward while also maximizing entropy. That is, to succeed at
the task while acting as randomly as possible. Prior deep RL methods based on
this framework have been formulated as Q-learning methods. By combining
off-policy updates with a stable stochastic actor-critic formulation, our
method achieves state-of-the-art performance on a range of continuous control
benchmark tasks, outperforming prior on-policy and off-policy methods.
Furthermore, we demonstrate that, in contrast to other off-policy algorithms,
our approach is very stable, achieving very similar performance across
different random seeds.

keyInsights: The maximum entropy objective encourages stochastic policies, leading to improved exploration, increased robustness to errors, and the ability to capture multiple modes of optimal behavior.
Combining off-policy learning with a stable stochastic actor-critic formulation can achieve both sample efficiency and robust performance.
Employing two Q-functions and taking their minimum for value estimation helps mitigate overestimation bias, a common source of instability in off-policy deep RL.
originalProblem: Deep Reinforcement Learning (DRL) algorithms for continuous control faced high sample complexity, making them impractical for real-world applications where data collection is costly.
Many DRL algorithms, particularly off-policy methods like DDPG, exhibited brittle convergence and high sensitivity to hyperparameters, requiring extensive tuning.
Prior maximum entropy RL methods either lacked sample efficiency or struggled with complex approximate inference in continuous action spaces.
results: SAC achieved state-of-the-art performance on challenging continuous control tasks, including complex high-dimensional environments like Ant-v1 and Humanoid.
It demonstrated excellent sample efficiency, learning significantly faster than on-policy methods like PPO and outperforming DDPG.
The algorithm exhibited high stability and robustness across various random seeds, showing significantly less variability in performance compared to DDPG.
solution: Develops an off-policy actor-critic algorithm that incorporates the maximum entropy objective, balancing reward maximization with policy entropy.
Utilizes three neural networks: a state value function, two soft Q-functions (minimizing over the pair to mitigate overestimation), and a stochastic policy network.
Employs the reparameterization trick for low-variance policy gradients and a tanh squashing function to map actions to a bounded interval.
paper_summary.summary: Soft Actor-Critic (SAC), developed by researchers at BAIR lab, introduces an off-policy maximum entropy deep reinforcement learning algorithm for continuous control. It achieves state-of-the-art performance and high stability across various tasks while demonstrating excellent sample efficiency.

title:
Soft Actor-Critic: Off-Policy Maximum Entropy Deep Reinforcement
  Learning with a Stochastic Actor

abstract: Reinforcement Learning (RL) has achieved impressive results in robotics, yet high-performing pipelines remain highly task-specific, with little reuse of prior data. Offline Model-based RL (MBRL) offers greater data efficiency by training policies entirely from existing datasets, but suffers from compounding errors and distribution shift in long-horizon rollouts. Although existing methods have shown success in controlled simulation benchmarks, robustly applying them to the noisy, biased, and partially observed datasets typical of real-world robotics remains challenging. We present a principled pipeline for making offline MBRL effective on physical robots. Our RWM-U extends autoregressive world models with epistemic uncertainty estimation, enabling temporally consistent multi-step rollouts with uncertainty effectively propagated over long horizons. We combine RWM-U with MOPO-PPO, which adapts uncertainty-penalized policy optimization to the stable, on-policy PPO framework for real-world control. We evaluate our approach on diverse manipulation and locomotion tasks in simulation and on real quadruped and humanoid, training policies entirely from offline datasets. The resulting policies consistently outperform model-free and uncertainty-unaware model-based baselines, and fusing real-world data in model learning further yields robust policies that surpass online model-free baselines trained solely in simulation.

keyInsights: The epistemic uncertainty estimated by RWM-U, calculated as ensemble disagreement, reliably correlates with the true long-horizon prediction errors of the dynamics model.

The uncertainty penalty coefficient in MOPO-PPO is a critical hyperparameter, where a balanced value (e.g., ".0") enables robust policies by preventing overfitting to model inaccuracies while still allowing for effective exploration.
Combining a moderate amount of real-world offline data with simulated data yields the most robust policies, effectively bridging the sim-to-real gap while leveraging diverse experience.
originalProblem: Offline Reinforcement Learning (RL) faces challenges like distribution shift and compounding errors when learning from fixed datasets, limiting its ability to discover novel, high-performing behaviors.

Model-Based Reinforcement Learning (MBRL) in offline settings struggles with dynamics model inaccuracies accumulating over long horizons, leading to unreliable synthetic data and suboptimal policy learning.
Existing offline MBRL methods are predominantly evaluated in simulation or with simpler control tasks, lacking robust transferability to complex, long-horizon real-world robot locomotion.
results: RWM-U reliably estimates epistemic uncertainty, which closely correlates with the model's actual long-horizon prediction errors, confirming its use as a trust metric.

MOPO-PPO consistently outperforms model-free (CQL) and uncertainty-unaware model-based (MBPO, MBPO-PPO) baselines across various simulated robotic tasks, particularly in complex quadruped and humanoid locomotion.

Policies trained entirely offline using a combination of simulated and real-world data achieved the highest performance (e.g., 0.91 ".03 normalized reward on ANYmal D), surpassing state-of-the-art online model-free baselines and demonstrating successful deployment on physical ANYmal D and Unitree G1 robots.
solution: An Uncertainty-Aware Robotic World Model (RWM-U) extends previous work by incorporating an ensemble-based method to quantify both aleatoric and critical epistemic uncertainty in its autoregressive dynamics predictions.

Model-Based Offline Policy Optimization (MOPO-PPO) adapts the MOPO framework to Proximal Policy Optimization (PPO), a stable algorithm for robot control, by penalizing rewards with RWM-U's epistemic uncertainty.

The integrated pipeline trains RWM-U entirely offline, then iteratively uses it to generate uncertainty-penalized imagined trajectories for PPO policy optimization, steering the policy away from unreliable model predictions.
paper_summary.summary: Researchers at ETH Zurich developed a pipeline for offline Model-Based Reinforcement Learning that effectively trains and deploys policies on physical quadruped and humanoid robots, utilizing an Uncertainty-Aware Robotic World Model and uncertainty-penalized policy optimization. This approach yields policies from fixed datasets that achieve robust performance on real hardware, even surpassing online model-free baselines when combining simulated and real-world offline data.

title: Uncertainty-Aware Robotic World Model Makes Offline Model-Based Reinforcement Learning Work on Real Robots

abstract:
Reinforcement learning (RL) has driven significant progress in robotics, but
its complexity and long training times remain major bottlenecks. In this
report, we introduce FastTD3, a simple, fast, and capable RL algorithm that
significantly speeds up training for humanoid robots in popular suites such as
HumanoidBench, IsaacLab, and MuJoCo Playground. Our recipe is remarkably
simple: we train an off-policy TD3 agent with several modifications -- parallel
simulation, large-batch updates, a distributional critic, and carefully tuned
hyperparameters. FastTD3 solves a range of HumanoidBench tasks in under 3 hours
on a single A100 GPU, while remaining stable during training. We also provide a
lightweight and easy-to-use implementation of FastTD3 to accelerate RL research
in robotics.

keyInsights: State-of-the-art wall-clock performance for complex robotic control can be achieved by efficiently scaling well-understood off-policy RL components rather than relying on novel complex algorithms.
Massively parallel environments and large-batch training stabilize and accelerate off-policy learning, reducing the need for architectural complexities like residual paths or layer normalization.

Rapid training times enable faster iteration on reward functions, revealing that different RL algorithms may require specific reward tunings for deployable policies, as demonstrated by the distinct human-like gaits achieved with custom rewards for FastTD3 vs. PPO on humanoid locomotion tasks.

Optimizing the implementation with mixed-precision training and `torch.compile` provides substantial speedups, up to 70% in combined optimizations, contributing significantly to reduced wall-clock training times.
originalProblem: Reinforcement Learning (RL) for complex robotic systems, such as humanoids, faces prohibitively long training times and high algorithmic complexity, hindering rapid iteration during reward design.

On-policy RL algorithms (e.g., PPO) are fast with parallel simulation but sample-inefficient, while off-policy RL algorithms (e.g., SAC, TD3) are sample-efficient but often slower in wall-clock time and more complex.
Many state-of-the-art RL algorithms fail to solve challenging benchmark tasks, like those in HumanoidBench, even after extended training durations (e.g., 48 hours).
results: FastTD3 consistently outperforms state-of-the-art baselines (PPO, SAC, SimbaV2, TDMPC2, DreamerV3) in wall-clock time across HumanoidBench, IsaacLab, and MuJoCo Playground benchmarks.
It solves a range of HumanoidBench tasks in under 3 hours on a single A100 GPU, demonstrating superior training speed and capability.

FastTD3 successfully transferred a policy trained in MuJoCo Playground to a real-world Booster T1 humanoid robot, marking a documented instance of off-policy RL policy deployment on a full-size humanoid.

Ablation studies confirmed that massively parallel environments, large batch sizes (32,768), and a distributional critic are key components contributing to the algorithm's speed and performance, while complex architectural stabilizers were found to be unnecessary and sometimes detrimental to speed.
solution: FastTD3 builds upon the Twin Delayed Deep Deterministic Policy Gradient (TD3) algorithm, integrating massively parallel environments for accelerated data collection.
It utilizes unusually large batch sizes (32,768) and incorporates a distributional critic (C51) to enhance learning stability and efficiency.

The approach emphasizes optimized implementation through PyTorch features like mixed-precision training and `torch.compile` for significant speedups, coupled with careful hyperparameter tuning, rather than introducing new algorithmic complexities.
The system avoids complex architectural additions such as residual paths or layer normalization, leveraging data diversity from parallel simulation and large batches to maintain stability.
paper_summary.summary: FastTD3, developed by researchers at UC Berkeley including Pieter Abbeel, is an optimized off-policy reinforcement learning algorithm based on TD3. It achieves state-of-the-art wall-clock training times for complex robotic tasks, solving many HumanoidBench tasks in under 3 hours on a single A100 GPU, and demonstrated successful sim-to-real transfer to a physical humanoid robot.

title: FastTD3: Simple, Fast, and Capable Reinforcement Learning for Humanoid  Control

abstract: Recent advances in learning decision-making policies can largely be attributed to training expressive policy models, largely via imitation learning. While imitation learning discards non-expert data, reinforcement learning (RL) can still learn from suboptimal data. However, instantiating RL training of a new policy class often presents a different challenge: most deep RL machinery is co-developed with assumptions on the policy class and backbone, resulting in poor performance when the policy class changes. For instance, SAC utilizes a low-variance reparameterization policy gradient for Gaussian policies, but this is unstable for diffusion policies and intractable for autoregressive categorical policies. To address this issue, we develop an offline RL and online fine-tuning approach called policy-agnostic RL (PA-RL) that can effectively train multiple policy classes, with varying architectures and sizes. We build off the basic idea that a universal supervised learning loss can replace the policy improvement step in RL, as long as it is applied on "optimized" actions. To obtain these optimized actions, we first sample multiple actions from a base policy, and run global optimization (i.e., re-ranking multiple action samples using the Q-function) and local optimization (i.e., running gradient steps on an action sample) to maximize the critic on these candidates. PA-RL enables fine-tuning diffusion and transformer policies with either autoregressive tokens or continuous action outputs, at different sizes, entirely via actor-critic RL. Moreover, PA-RL improves the performance and sample-efficiency by up to 2 times compared to existing offline RL and online fine-tuning methods. We show the first result that successfully fine-tunes OpenVLA, a 7B generalist robot policy, autonomously with Cal-QL, an online RL fine-tuning algorithm, improving from 40% to 70% in the real world in 40 minutes.

keyInsights: Policy Agnostic Reinforcement Learning (PA-RL) decouples policy improvement from policy training, optimizing actions directly before distilling them into the policy.
A two-stage action optimization process combines global selection of high-Q-value actions with local gradient ascent to refine them.

The method uses supervised learning for policy updates, allowing it to support any policy class that can be trained with a supervised objective, including expressive models like diffusion policies and autoregressive transformers.
originalProblem: Traditional reinforcement learning algorithms are tightly coupled with specific policy classes, like Gaussian policies, limiting their flexibility.
Existing RL methods struggle to leverage more expressive policy representations such as diffusion models or transformers without significant algorithm modifications.
There is a need for RL algorithms that can work with any policy class and backbone architecture to fully utilize diverse policy representations.
results: PA-RL with diffusion policies achieved a normalized average score improvement of 79% to 100% in D4RL Kitchen tasks, compared to 43% for Gaussian Cal-QL.
It achieved near-perfect performance in the challenging D4RL AntMaze-large-diverse-v2 environment, substantially outperforming traditional methods.

PA-RL successfully fine-tuned diffusion policies on real robots, improving cup retrieval success rates from 50% to 95%, and increased OpenVLA policy success rates from 40% to 70% in vegetable manipulation tasks with minimal online fine-tuning.
solution: PA-RL introduces a framework that decouples the reinforcement learning algorithm from the policy class.
It optimizes actions directly by sampling, filtering by Q-values, and applying gradient ascent, then distills these optimized actions into the policy via supervised learning.
The approach integrates with existing actor-critic RL methods (e.g., Cal-QL, IQL) by replacing their policy improvement steps with this action optimization and distillation procedure.
paper_summary.summary: This paper introduces Policy Agnostic Reinforcement Learning (PA-RL), a novel framework that decouples the RL algorithm from the policy class, enabling effective training of diverse policy types including Gaussian, diffusion, and transformer policies. PA-RL achieved substantial performance gains in simulated D4RL benchmarks and demonstrated successful fine-tuning of expressive policies like Diffusion Policies and OpenVLA on real-world robotic manipulation tasks.

title: Policy Agnostic RL: Offline RL and Online RL Fine-Tuning of Any Class and Backbone

abstract: Massively parallel simulation has reduced reinforcement learning (RL) training time for robots from days to minutes. However, achieving fast and reliable sim-to-real RL for humanoid control remains difficult due to the challenges introduced by factors such as high dimensionality and domain randomization. In this work, we introduce a simple and practical recipe based on off-policy RL algorithms, i.e., FastSAC and FastTD3, that enables rapid training of humanoid locomotion policies in just 15 minutes with a single RTX 4090 GPU. Our simple recipe stabilizes off-policy RL algorithms at massive scale with thousands of parallel environments through carefully tuned design choices and minimalist reward functions. We demonstrate rapid end-to-end learning of humanoid locomotion controllers on Unitree G1 and Booster T1 robots under strong domain randomization, e.g., randomized dynamics, rough terrain, and push perturbations, as well as fast training of whole-body human-motion tracking policies. We provide videos and open-source implementation at: this https URL.

keyInsights: Off-policy RL algorithms, when properly scaled and tuned, can achieve significantly faster wall-clock training times than on-policy methods for robust, high-dimensional humanoid control.
Robust and natural humanoid behaviors can emerge from minimalist reward functions, challenging the need for complex reward shaping and streamlining policy development.

Critical design elements like layer normalization, specific critic configurations (e.g., average of Q-values), and refined exploration strategies (e.g., auto-tuning alpha) are essential for stability and performance in complex humanoid tasks.
originalProblem: Sim-to-real transfer for complex humanoid robots demands extensive domain randomization and complex reward shaping, often leading to prohibitively slow training times.
The iterative development cycles for real-world robotic policies become impractical and expensive when each training iteration takes hours or days.
On-policy RL algorithms like PPO, while commonly used, exhibit sample inefficiency that contributes to lengthy training for high-dimensional humanoid tasks.
results: Humanoid locomotion policies for Unitree G1 and Booster T1 robots are trained in 15 minutes on a single RTX 4090 GPU, showing substantial speed improvements over PPO.
The learned policies demonstrate robust locomotion on rough terrain and under consistent external push perturbations, as well as effective whole-body motion tracking for complex sequences like dance.
Successfully deployed policies on real Unitree G1 hardware exhibit robust locomotion and complex whole-body motions (e.g., 2-minute dance) for extended durations.
solution: A "simple and practical recipe" that integrates efficient off-policy reinforcement learning algorithms (FastSAC, FastTD3) with massively parallel simulation.
Carefully tuned design choices include joint-limit-aware action bounds, observation and layer normalization, and refined critic and exploration hyperparameters.
A minimalist reward design philosophy uses fewer than 10 essential terms, supported by curriculum learning and symmetry augmentation, to simplify policy optimization.
paper_summary.summary: A methodology from Amazon FAR, featuring efficient off-policy reinforcement learning and massively parallel simulation, enables training robust sim-to-real humanoid locomotion policies in just 15 minutes. This approach demonstrates successful deployment on real Unitree G1 hardware and yields policies for full-body locomotion and complex motion tracking using minimalist reward functions.

title: Learning Sim-to-Real Humanoid Locomotion in 15 Minutes

abstract:
Model-free deep reinforcement learning (RL) algorithms have been successfully
applied to a range of challenging sequential decision making and control tasks.
However, these methods typically suffer from two major challenges: high sample
complexity and brittleness to hyperparameters. Both of these challenges limit
the applicability of such methods to real-world domains. In this paper, we
describe Soft Actor-Critic (SAC), our recently introduced off-policy
actor-critic algorithm based on the maximum entropy RL framework. In this
framework, the actor aims to simultaneously maximize expected return and
entropy. That is, to succeed at the task while acting as randomly as possible.
We extend SAC to incorporate a number of modifications that accelerate training
and improve stability with respect to the hyperparameters, including a
constrained formulation that automatically tunes the temperature
hyperparameter. We systematically evaluate SAC on a range of benchmark tasks,
as well as real-world challenging tasks such as locomotion for a quadrupedal
robot and robotic manipulation with a dexterous hand. With these improvements,
SAC achieves state-of-the-art performance, outperforming prior on-policy and
off-policy methods in sample-efficiency and asymptotic performance.
Furthermore, we demonstrate that, in contrast to other off-policy algorithms,
our approach is very stable, achieving similar performance across different
random seeds. These results suggest that SAC is a promising candidate for
learning in real-world robotics tasks.

keyInsights: Maximum entropy reinforcement learning enhances exploration and robustness, leading to more stable and generalizable policies.
Automating the temperature hyperparameter tuning via a dual objective significantly improves algorithm stability and practical usability, removing a major hurdle for real-world deployment.

The combination of off-policy learning, actor-critic architecture, reparameterization trick, and entropy regularization creates a highly sample-efficient and robust deep reinforcement learning algorithm for continuous control.
originalProblem: Model-free deep reinforcement learning algorithms suffered from high sample complexity, making them impractical for data-expensive real-world applications like robotics.
Existing deep RL methods, especially off-policy ones, were often brittle and highly sensitive to hyperparameter tuning, requiring extensive manual effort for each new task.
Learning complex robotic skills directly on physical hardware, especially from raw sensor data, was exceptionally challenging due to data scarcity and algorithm instability.
results: SAC achieved state-of-the-art sample efficiency and asymptotic performance on simulated continuous control benchmarks (e.g., Ant-v2, Humanoid-v2), outperforming DDPG, PPO, and TD3.
A Minitaur robot learned a robust walking gait directly in the real world in approximately 160,000 environment steps (2 hours), generalizing to unseen terrains like slopes and obstacles.

A dexterous robotic hand learned to rotate a valve from raw RGB images in about 300,000 environment steps (20 hours), demonstrating efficient end-to-end learning for complex manipulation tasks on physical hardware.
solution: Introduced Soft Actor-Critic (SAC), an off-policy deep reinforcement learning algorithm based on the maximum entropy framework.
Utilized an actor-critic architecture with neural networks for policy and Q-functions, alongside an experience replay buffer for efficient data reuse.
Developed an automatic, gradient-based temperature tuning mechanism that dynamically adjusts the entropy regularization weight, eliminating the need for manual hyperparameter tuning.
paper_summary.summary: This paper presents Soft Actor-Critic (SAC), an off-policy deep reinforcement learning algorithm for continuous control that combines maximum entropy principles with an actor-critic architecture and a novel automatic temperature tuning mechanism. SAC achieves state-of-the-art sample efficiency and robust performance across simulated benchmarks and demonstrates unprecedented real-world capabilities, such as learning quadrupedal locomotion in two hours and dexterous manipulation from raw images.

title: Soft Actor-Critic Algorithms and Applications

abstract: Score-function based methods for policy learning, such as REINFORCE and PPO, have delivered strong results in game-playing and robotics, yet their high variance often undermines training stability. Using pathwise policy gradients, i.e. computing a derivative by differentiating the objective function, alleviates the variance issues. However, they require an accurate action-conditioned value function, which is notoriously hard to learn without relying on replay buffers for reusing past off-policy data. We present an on-policy algorithm that trains Q-value models purely from on-policy trajectories, unlocking the possibility of using pathwise policy updates in the context of on-policy learning. We show how to combine stochastic policies for exploration with constrained updates for stable training, and evaluate important architectural components that stabilize value function learning. The result, Relative Entropy Pathwise Policy Optimization (REPPO), is an efficient on-policy algorithm that combines the stability of pathwise policy gradients with the simplicity and minimal memory footprint of standard on-policy learning. Compared to state-of-the-art on two standard GPU-parallelized benchmarks, REPPO provides strong empirical performance at superior sample efficiency, wall-clock time, memory footprint, and hyperparameter robustness.

keyInsights: Robust surrogate value functions can be learned effectively from purely on-policy data, enabling stable pathwise policy gradient estimation without the need for large replay buffers.

The integration of maximum entropy regularization and KL divergence constraints with dynamic coefficient tuning is crucial for achieving stable and explorative policy updates in an on-policy pathwise setting.
Architectural advancements, specifically categorical Q-learning via HL-Gauss loss and layer normalization, significantly contribute to stabilizing on-policy value function learning.
originalProblem: Score-function-based on-policy reinforcement learning algorithms (e.g., PPO) suffer from high variance in gradient estimates, leading to unstable training.

Pathwise policy gradient methods (e.g., DDPG, SAC) traditionally require off-policy data collection with large replay buffers for accurate Q-function learning, incurring high memory costs and distribution shift challenges.
A challenge exists in combining the low-variance benefits of pathwise gradients with the memory efficiency and theoretical simplicity of on-policy learning without relying on large replay buffers.
results: REPPO demonstrates superior sample efficiency, achieving higher performance levels faster than PPO on MuJoCo Playground and ManiSkill benchmarks, while matching PPO's wall-clock training speed.
The algorithm significantly reduces memory footprint by two to three orders of magnitude compared to off-policy methods like FastTD3, making it suitable for resource-constrained environments.
REPPO exhibits enhanced training stability and hyperparameter robustness, performing consistently across more than 30 tasks with a single set of hyperparameters.
solution: REPPO develops an on-policy actor-critic algorithm that uses a pathwise policy gradient estimator to update the policy directly through a learned Q-function.
It learns a robust surrogate Q-function purely from on-policy data, employing multi-step TD-λ targets, a maximum entropy formulation, and the categorical HL-Gauss loss for stable value estimation.

The policy update incorporates KL-constrained policy changes and dynamically tunes both entropy and KL regularization coefficients to balance exploration and ensure stable, controlled policy progression.
paper_summary.summary: The REPPO algorithm introduces an on-policy reinforcement learning approach that leverages low-variance pathwise policy gradients by learning a robust surrogate Q-function purely from on-policy data. This method demonstrates superior sample efficiency and training stability on benchmarks while requiring significantly less memory than off-policy alternatives.

title: Relative Entropy Pathwise Policy Optimization

abstract: Reinforcement learning in massively parallel physics simulations has driven major progress in sim-to-real robot learning. However, current approaches remain brittle and task-specific, relying on extensive per-task engineering to design rewards, curricula, and demonstrations. Even with this engineering, they often fail on long-horizon, contact-rich manipulation tasks and do not meaningfully scale with compute, as performance quickly saturates when training revisits the same narrow regions of state space. We introduce OmniReset, a simple and scalable framework that enables on-policy reinforcement learning to robustly solve a broad class of dexterous manipulation tasks using a single reward function, fixed algorithm hyperparameters, no curricula, and no human demonstrations. Our key insight is that long-horizon exploration can be dramatically simplified by using simulator resets to systematically expose the RL algorithm to the diverse set of robot-object interactions which underlie dexterous manipulation. OmniReset programmatically generates such resets with minimal human input, converting additional compute directly into broader behavioral coverage and continued performance gains. We show that OmniReset gracefully scales to long-horizon dexterous manipulation tasks beyond the capabilities of existing approaches and is able to learn robust policies over significantly wider ranges of initial conditions than baselines. Finally, we distill OmniReset into visuomotor policies which display robust retrying behavior and substantially higher success rates than baselines when transferred to the real world zero-shot. Project webpage: this https URL

keyInsights: Systematically exposing RL agents to a comprehensive array of robot-object interaction modes through diverse simulator resets significantly simplifies long-horizon exploration.

Massively parallel reinforcement learning with sparse rewards can induce emergent, complex dexterous behaviors, including multi-stage strategies, without explicit human-engineered curricula or demonstrations.

Zero-shot sim-to-real transfer for complex manipulation is facilitated by distilling expert policies and employing extensive visual and dynamic randomizations, alongside precise system identification.
originalProblem: Robotic reinforcement learning for dexterous manipulation often necessitates extensive human engineering, including task-specific reward shaping, curriculum design, or human demonstrations.
Exploration in complex, contact-rich manipulation tasks quickly saturates with standard RL methods, hindering performance scaling even with increased computational resources.
Transferring learned manipulation policies from simulation to the real world zero-shot remains challenging due to the sim-to-real gap and lack of robustness.
results: OmniReset achieved high simulation success rates across various contact-rich tasks, like Leg Twisting and Peg Insertion, consistently surpassing baseline methods on challenging long-horizon scenarios.
Increased parallel environments directly correlated with improved learning performance and stability in simulation, showcasing the framework's scalability with computational resources.

Visuomotor policies, distilled from simulation, demonstrated robust zero-shot real-world transfer with 85.37% success on Peg Insertion and 56.36% on Leg Twisting, significantly outperforming a diffusion policy baseline.
solution: An on-policy reinforcement learning framework, OmniReset, programmatically generates diverse initial state distributions (resets) to cover a wide range of robot-object interactions and pathways to the goal.
It employs a single, task-agnostic reward function, PPO with generalized state-dependent exploration noise (gSDE), and leverages massively parallel simulation environments.
State-based policies are distilled into visuomotor policies using large datasets and extensive sim-to-real randomizations for robust zero-shot deployment on physical robots.
paper_summary.summary: Large-scale reinforcement learning, facilitated by the OmniReset framework, acquires emergent dexterous manipulation skills for diverse contact-rich tasks using programmatically generated simulator resets. This approach demonstrates high simulation success rates and enables zero-shot real-world transfer, achieving an 85.37% success rate for Peg Insertion on a physical robot.

title: Emergent Dexterity via Diverse Resets and Large-Scale Reinforcement Learning

abstract:
Reinforcement learning (RL) provides an appealing formalism for learning
control policies from experience. However, the classic active formulation of RL
necessitates a lengthy active exploration process for each behavior, making it
difficult to apply in real-world settings such as robotic control. If we can
instead allow RL algorithms to effectively use previously collected data to aid
the online learning process, such applications could be made substantially more
practical: the prior data would provide a starting point that mitigates
challenges due to exploration and sample complexity, while the online training
enables the agent to perfect the desired skill. Such prior data could either
constitute expert demonstrations or sub-optimal prior data that illustrates
potentially useful transitions. While a number of prior methods have either
used optimal demonstrations to bootstrap RL, or have used sub-optimal data to
train purely offline, it remains exceptionally difficult to train a policy with
offline data and actually continue to improve it further with online RL. In
this paper we analyze why this problem is so challenging, and propose an
algorithm that combines sample efficient dynamic programming with maximum
likelihood policy updates, providing a simple and effective framework that is
able to leverage large amounts of offline data and then quickly perform online
fine-tuning of RL policies. We show that our method, advantage weighted actor
critic (AWAC), enables rapid learning of skills with a combination of prior
demonstration data and online experience. We demonstrate these benefits on
simulated and real-world robotics domains, including dexterous manipulation
with a real multi-fingered hand, drawer opening with a robotic arm, and
rotating a valve. Our results show that incorporating prior data can reduce the
time required to learn a range of robotic skills to practical time-scales.

keyInsights: Explicitly modeling the behavior policy (π_β) in offline RL methods leads to excessive conservatism during online fine-tuning as the true behavior distribution shifts.

An implicit constraint on the policy update, derived from a KKT condition, allows effective leveraging of offline data without the instability of unconstrained off-policy methods or the conservatism of explicit behavior modeling.

Using the current policy's Q-function for advantage estimation within an off-policy critic provides a more sample-efficient and asymptotically better performance than Monte-Carlo or TD(λ) based approaches.
originalProblem: Traditional online reinforcement learning requires vast amounts of costly and time-consuming data, especially for real-world robotics.

Existing methods struggle to effectively combine offline pre-training with online fine-tuning: off-policy methods are unstable, while pure offline methods are overly conservative during online adaptation.
Robots face significant challenges with exploration in sparse-reward environments, leading to high sample complexity and safety concerns.
results: AWAC significantly outperforms prior methods on challenging simulated dexterous manipulation tasks, learning tasks like pen rotation in 120K timesteps where others fail.

On real-world robotic tasks (valve rotation, drawer opening, object repositioning), AWAC achieves strong initial performance after offline pre-training and consistently improves with limited online interaction.

The algorithm demonstrates robustness to data quality, effectively learning even when initialized with datasets generated by a random policy and outperforming baselines on D4RL benchmarks across varying data qualities.
solution: AWAC uses an actor-critic framework with a standard off-policy temporal difference (TD) learning update for the critic, enabling efficient data reuse.
The actor is updated using an advantage-weighted maximum likelihood objective, implicitly enforcing a policy constraint without explicitly modeling the behavior policy of the offline data.
The algorithm iteratively updates the critic and actor, starting with offline data for pre-training and then seamlessly integrating newly collected online data for continuous fine-tuning.
paper_summary.summary: A new reinforcement learning algorithm, AWAC, enables robust pre-training from diverse offline datasets and efficient online fine-tuning for continuous improvement. It significantly accelerates learning on complex simulated and real-world robotic manipulation tasks, often outperforming prior methods that struggle with data efficiency or online adaptation.

title: AWAC: Accelerating Online Reinforcement Learning with Offline Datasets

abstract: Visual reinforcement learning is appealing for robotics but expensive -- off-policy methods are sample-efficient yet slow; on-policy methods parallelize well but waste samples. Recent work has shown that off-policy methods can train faster than on-policy methods in wall-clock time for state-based control. Extending this to vision remains challenging, where high-dimensional input images complicate training dynamics and introduce substantial storage and encoding overhead. To address these challenges, we introduce Squint, a visual Soft Actor Critic method that achieves faster wall-clock training than prior visual off-policy and on-policy methods. Squint achieves this via parallel simulation, a distributional critic, resolution squinting, layer normalization, a tuned update-to-data ratio, and an optimized implementation. We evaluate on the SO-101 Task Set, a new suite of eight manipulation tasks in ManiSkill3 with heavy domain randomization, and demonstrate sim-to-real transfer to a real SO-101 robot. We train policies for 15 minutes on a single RTX 3090 GPU, with most tasks converging in under 6 minutes.

keyInsights: Optimized off-policy visual RL, specifically Squint, can achieve superior wall-clock training speed compared to both prior visual off-policy and on-policy methods, even with high-dimensional visual inputs and heavy domain randomization.

Architectural choices like using a C51 distributional critic and image preprocessing techniques such as "resolution squinting" are crucial for accelerating training while maintaining high performance in visual RL.
Aggressive visual and physical domain randomization is essential for enabling robust zero-shot sim-to-real transfer of visual policies trained quickly in simulation.
originalProblem: Visual reinforcement learning (visual RL) is computationally expensive, requiring millions of environment interactions and substantial wall-clock training time, hindering practical deployment on physical robots.
While off-policy RL methods are sample-efficient, their computational overhead often leads to longer wall-clock times, especially with high-dimensional image inputs.
On-policy methods, though strong in parallelized simulations for state-based control, exhibit poor sample efficiency and struggle with complex visual tasks and heavy domain randomization.
results: Squint achieved an average 96.1% success rate in simulation and 91.3% success in zero-shot real-world deployment on eight diverse robotic manipulation tasks after only 15 minutes of training.

The method significantly outperformed optimized SAC, PPO, and DrQ-v2 baselines in terms of wall-clock training speed and final success rates, demonstrating convergence for most tasks in under 6 minutes.
Ablation studies confirmed that the C51 distributional critic and "resolution squinting" were critical for improving wall-time speed and task performance, respectively, validating the design choices.
solution: Squint is an optimized visual Soft Actor Critic (SAC) method that leverages GPU-accelerated parallel simulation (ManiSkill3) for massive data collection and a specific Update-to-Data (UTD) ratio.

The method incorporates PyTorch optimizations (e.g., torch.compile, cudagraphs, AMP bfloat16) to achieve over 5x speedup in the training loop, along with a C51 distributional critic and layer normalization.

It employs "resolution squinting," rendering images at higher resolution (128x128) then area-downsampling to 16x16 input, which preserves scene structure and aids sim-to-real transfer, alongside a large GPU-resident replay buffer.
paper_summary.summary: Researchers at the University of California San Diego developed Squint, an optimized visual Soft Actor Critic (SAC) method that dramatically reduces training time for sim-to-real robotics. Squint achieves an average 96.1% success rate in simulation and 91.3% in zero-shot real-world deployment on eight manipulation tasks after just 15 minutes of training on a single GPU.

title: Squint: Fast Visual Reinforcement Learning for Sim-to-Real Robotics

abstract: Reinforcement learning (RL) is a powerful paradigm for learning to make sequences of decisions. However, RL has yet to be fully leveraged in robotics, principally due to its lack of scalability. Offline RL offers a promising avenue by training agents on large, diverse datasets, avoiding the costly real-world interactions of online RL. Scaling offline RL to increasingly complex datasets requires expressive generative models such as diffusion and flow matching. However, existing methods typically depend on either backpropagation through time (BPTT), which is computationally prohibitive, or policy distillation, which introduces compounding errors and limits scalability to larger base policies. In this paper, we consider the question of how to develop a scalable offline RL approach without relying on distillation or backpropagation through time. We introduce Expressive Value Learning for Offline Reinforcement Learning (EVOR): a scalable offline RL approach that integrates both expressive policies and expressive value functions. EVOR learns an optimal, regularized Q-function via flow matching during training. At inference-time, EVOR performs inference-time policy extraction via rejection sampling against the expressive value function, enabling efficient optimization, regularization, and compute-scalable search without retraining. Empirically, we show that EVOR outperforms baselines on a diverse set of offline RL tasks, demonstrating the benefit of integrating expressive value learning into offline RL.

keyInsights: The expressivity of generative models, like flow matching, can be applied not only to policies but also to value functions to better capture complex reward-to-go distributions in offline RL.
Learning an optimal, regularized distributional Q-function from an expressive generative model provides a robust theoretical foundation for offline policy evaluation and extraction.

Effective inference-time policy extraction through rejection sampling with an expressive value function can bypass the computational limitations of BPTT and policy distillation in large-scale offline RL.
originalProblem: Offline Reinforcement Learning (RL) for robotics faces scalability issues due to the high computational cost of Backpropagation Through Time (BPTT) for generative policies.
Policy distillation, an alternative to BPTT, sacrifices policy expressivity and introduces compounding errors.
Traditional MLP-based value functions lack the expressivity to model complex reward-to-go distributions from diverse offline datasets.
Existing inference-time policy extraction methods using rejection sampling are limited by unregularized and less expressive value functions.
results: EVOR consistently outperformed baselines (Q-chunking) across all five environments and 25 distinct tasks in the OGBench robotics suite, showing up to 50 ± 4 success rate on Antmaze-large-navigate-singletask.

The method demonstrated effective inference-time scaling, with performance improving as the number of action candidates increased, validating its ability to leverage additional compute without retraining.

EVOR achieved robust performance across diverse robotics tasks using a single set of training and evaluation hyperparameters, simplifying deployment and reducing tuning overhead compared to other methods.
solution: EVOR learns a base policy through behavioral cloning using flow matching to capture complex and multi-modal action distributions.
It develops a flow-based distributional Temporal Difference (TD) learning approach to train an expressive, optimal, and regularized Q-function that models the full reward-to-go distribution.
Inference-time policy extraction is performed via rejection sampling, where multiple action candidates from the base policy are evaluated by the expressive Q-function, avoiding BPTT and distillation.
The framework allows for dynamic scaling of performance and regularization at inference time by adjusting the number of action candidates and temperature parameters without retraining.
paper_summary.summary: EVOR introduces expressive value learning for offline reinforcement learning by integrating flow matching into distributional temporal difference (TD) learning. This framework learns an optimal, regularized Q-function that enables scalable inference-time policy extraction, achieving superior performance on OGBench robotics tasks with minimal hyperparameter tuning.

title: Expressive Value Learning for Scalable Offline Reinforcement Learning

abstract: Despite the considerable potential of reinforcement learning (RL), robotic control tasks predominantly rely on imitation learning (IL) due to its better sample efficiency. However, it is costly to collect comprehensive expert demonstrations that enable IL to generalize to all possible scenarios, and any distribution shift would require recollecting data for finetuning. Therefore, RL is appealing if it can build upon IL as an efficient autonomous self-improvement procedure. We propose imitation bootstrapped reinforcement learning (IBRL), a novel framework for sample-efficient RL with demonstrations that first trains an IL policy on the provided demonstrations and then uses it to propose alternative actions for both online exploration and bootstrapping target values. Compared to prior works that oversample the demonstrations or regularize RL with an additional imitation loss, IBRL is able to utilize high quality actions from IL policies since the beginning of training, which greatly accelerates exploration and training efficiency. We evaluate IBRL on 6 simulation and 3 real-world tasks spanning various difficulty levels. IBRL significantly outperforms prior methods and the improvement is particularly more prominent in harder tasks.

keyInsights: Explicitly leveraging a pre-trained, standalone imitation learning policy for both action selection during online exploration and value bootstrapping during RL training significantly enhances sample efficiency and overall performance.

A modular design, allowing distinct architectures for the imitation learning policy and the reinforcement learning Q-network, enables each component to be optimized for its specific learning objective.

Architectural improvements like actor dropout and a Vision Transformer (ViT)-based Q-network provide general benefits for sample-efficient continuous control reinforcement learning, improving stability and performance across various methods.
originalProblem: Reinforcement learning (RL) in real-world robotics faces challenges due to high sample efficiency requirements and difficult exploration, especially with sparse rewards and continuous action spaces.
Imitation learning (IL) is sample-efficient but struggles with generalization to states outside the demonstration distribution, limiting autonomous improvement.

Existing methods combining RL with demonstrations often suffer from issues like requiring hyperparameter tuning, lack of architectural flexibility between IL and RL components, or insufficient integration of the IL policy's knowledge.
results: IBRL consistently matched or outperformed state-of-the-art baselines (e.g., RLPD, RFT, MoDem) across 6 simulation tasks and 3 challenging real-world robotic manipulation tasks.

In real-world robotic tasks, IBRL achieved 100% success in "Lift" (95% in hard evaluation where behavioral cloning failed), 95% in "Drawer" with 40% more sample efficiency than baselines, and 85% in "Hang," outperforming the next best RL method by 2.4x.

Ablation studies confirmed that both the actor proposal (IL policy for online exploration) and bootstrap proposal (IL policy for Q-value target estimation) mechanisms were crucial for IBRL's performance, with the bootstrap proposal contributing more substantially to sample efficiency and convergence.
solution: Pre-train a separate, high-capacity imitation policy using behavioral cloning on expert demonstrations.

Explicitly integrate this fixed IL policy into the online RL loop by using it to propose actions for both exploration (actor proposal) and target Q-value computation (bootstrap proposal), selecting the action with the higher Q-value alongside the RL policy's action.

Incorporate architectural and regularization enhancements, including actor dropout for stability and a novel Vision Transformer (ViT)-based Q-network for efficient visual processing in pixel-based tasks.
paper_summary.summary: Imitation Bootstrapped Reinforcement Learning (IBRL) from Stanford University integrates a pre-trained imitation learning policy into both the online exploration and RL training phases of a TD3 backbone. The method achieves state-of-the-art performance, demonstrating superior sample efficiency and success rates across various complex simulation and real-world robotic manipulation tasks, often outperforming prior methods by significant margins.

title: Imitation Bootstrapped Reinforcement Learning

abstract: Reinforcement learning (RL) has shown remarkable success in solving complex decision-making and control tasks. However, many model-free RL algorithms experience performance degradation due to inaccurate value estimation, particularly the overestimation of Q-values, which can lead to suboptimal policies. To address this issue, we previously proposed the Distributional Soft Actor-Critic (DSAC or DSACv1), an off-policy RL algorithm that enhances value estimation accuracy by learning a continuous Gaussian value distribution. Despite its effectiveness, DSACv1 faces challenges such as training instability and sensitivity to reward scaling, caused by high variance in critic gradients due to return randomness. In this paper, we introduce three key refinements to DSACv1 to overcome these limitations and further improve Q-value estimation accuracy: expected value substitution, twin value distribution learning, and variance-based critic gradient adjustment. The enhanced algorithm, termed DSAC with Three refinements (DSAC-T or DSACv2), is systematically evaluated across a diverse set of benchmark tasks. Without the need for task-specific hyperparameter tuning, DSAC-T consistently matches or outperforms leading model-free RL algorithms, including SAC, TD3, DDPG, TRPO, and PPO, in all tested environments. Additionally, DSAC-T ensures a stable learning process and maintains robust performance across varying reward scales. Its effectiveness is further demonstrated through real-world application in controlling a wheeled robot, highlighting its potential for deployment in practical robotic tasks.

organization_info.name: University of Science and Technology Beijing

title: Distributional Soft Actor-Critic with Three Refinements

abstract:
In reinforcement learning (RL), function approximation errors are known to
easily lead to the Q-value overestimations, thus greatly reducing policy
performance. This paper presents a distributional soft actor-critic (DSAC)
algorithm, which is an off-policy RL method for continuous control setting, to
improve the policy performance by mitigating Q-value overestimations. We first
discover in theory that learning a distribution function of state-action
returns can effectively mitigate Q-value overestimations because it is capable
of adaptively adjusting the update stepsize of the Q-value function. Then, a
distributional soft policy iteration (DSPI) framework is developed by embedding
the return distribution function into maximum entropy RL. Finally, we present a
deep off-policy actor-critic variant of DSPI, called DSAC, which directly
learns a continuous return distribution by keeping the variance of the
state-action returns within a reasonable range to address exploding and
vanishing gradient problems. We evaluate DSAC on the suite of MuJoCo continuous
control tasks, achieving the state-of-the-art performance.
title:
Distributional Soft Actor-Critic: Off-Policy Reinforcement Learning for
  Addressing Value Estimation Errors

abstract: Building capable household and industrial robots requires mastering the control of versatile, high-degree-of-freedom (DoF) systems such as mobile manipulators. While reinforcement learning (RL) holds promise for autonomously acquiring robot control policies, scaling it to high-DoF embodiments remains challenging. Direct RL in the real world demands both safe exploration and high sample efficiency, which are difficult to achieve in practice. Sim-to-real RL, on the other hand, is often brittle due to the reality gap. This paper introduces SLAC, a method that renders real-world RL feasible for complex embodiments by leveraging a low-fidelity simulator to pretrain a task-agnostic latent action space. SLAC trains this latent action space via a customized unsupervised skill discovery method designed to promote temporal abstraction, disentanglement, and safety, thereby facilitating efficient downstream learning. Once a latent action space is learned, SLAC uses it as the action interface for a novel off-policy RL algorithm to autonomously learn downstream tasks through real-world interactions. We evaluate SLAC against existing methods on a suite of bimanual mobile manipulation tasks, where it achieves state-of-the-art performance. Notably, SLAC learns contact-rich whole-body tasks in under an hour of real-world interactions, without relying on any demonstrations or hand-crafted behavior priors. More information and robot videos at this http URL

organization_info.name: The University of Texas at Austin

keyInsights: Pretraining a latent action space in low-fidelity simulation, rather than a full task policy, offers robustness to the reality gap and enables more efficient and safer real-world learning for complex robots.
A disentangled and temporally extended latent action space, explicitly designed with safety considerations, simplifies the downstream real-world control problem and improves exploration.

Algorithmic innovations in FLA-SAC, such as reduced batch size regularization and factored Q-functions, are crucial for achieving high sample efficiency when learning in large discrete latent action spaces directly in the real world.
originalProblem: Controlling high-degree-of-freedom (DoF) robots for complex, long-horizon, and contact-rich tasks in unstructured environments presents significant challenges.

Existing robot policy learning methods (imitation learning, sim-to-real, direct real-world RL) face limitations such as high demonstration requirements, brittleness due to the reality gap, or prohibitive sample inefficiency and safety concerns in physical environments.
Many successful robotic applications still depend on extensive human supervision, teleoperation, or hand-crafted motion priors, which limits autonomy and scalability.
results: SLAC achieved state-of-the-art success rates (e.g., 90% on 'Clean Whiteboard', 80% on 'Wipe Board over Obstacles') on challenging bimanual mobile manipulation tasks.

It demonstrated unprecedented sample efficiency, learning complex real-world tasks in under an hour (approximately 50 minutes or 30k low-level steps) without relying on demonstrations or hand-crafted priors.
The framework exhibited the lowest number of safety violations during real-world training compared to all baseline methods, validating its robust safety integration.
solution: SLAC employs a two-step procedure: first, an unsupervised learning phase in a low-fidelity simulator to acquire a task-agnostic, safe, and disentangled latent action space.
A novel reward function combines a mutual information objective for temporal extension and disentanglement with a universal safety reward to shape the latent action space.

For real-world task learning, a new off-policy RL algorithm, Factorized Latent-Action SAC (FLA-SAC), is introduced, featuring a high update-to-data ratio with reduced batch size, Gumbel-softmax for large discrete action spaces, and a factored Q-function decomposition.
paper_summary.summary: The SLAC framework, developed by researchers at UT Austin, integrates a simulation-pretrained, task-agnostic latent action space with a novel real-world reinforcement learning algorithm to facilitate efficient and safe learning for high-degree-of-freedom mobile manipulators. It allows robots to master complex, contact-rich tasks within an hour of real-world interaction, demonstrating superior success rates and reduced safety incidents.

title: SLAC: Simulation-Pretrained Latent Action Space for Whole-Body Real-World RL

abstract: Reinforcement learning (RL) is widely used for humanoid control, with on-policy methods such as Proximal Policy Optimization (PPO) enabling robust training via large-scale parallel simulation and, in some cases, zero-shot deployment to real robots. However, the low sample efficiency of on-policy algorithms limits safe adaptation to new environments. Although off-policy RL and model-based RL have shown improved sample efficiency, the gap between large-scale pretraining and efficient finetuning on humanoids still exists. In this paper, we find that off-policy Soft Actor-Critic (SAC), with large-batch update and a high Update-To-Data (UTD) ratio, reliably supports large-scale pretraining of humanoid locomotion policies, achieving zero-shot deployment on real robots. For adaptation, we demonstrate that these SAC-pretrained policies can be finetuned in new environments and out-of-distribution tasks using model-based methods. Data collection in the new environment executes a deterministic policy while stochastic exploration is instead confined to a physics-informed world model. This separation mitigates the risks of random exploration during adaptation while preserving exploratory coverage for improvement. Overall, the approach couples the wall-clock efficiency of large-scale simulation during pretraining with the sample efficiency of model-based learning during fine-tuning. For code and videos, see this https URL

keyInsights: Large-scale SAC pretraining, when optimized with JAX for massively parallel simulation, yields robust policies capable of zero-shot transfer to physical humanoids.
Incorporating physics priors into the world model significantly improves its predictive accuracy, generalization, and stability for humanoid dynamics, which is vital for reliable synthetic rollouts.
Confining stochastic exploration to a learned physics-informed world model enables safe and sample-efficient policy adaptation for humanoids without risky real-world random actions.
originalProblem: On-policy Reinforcement Learning methods for humanoid control suffer from low sample efficiency, making real-world adaptation costly and time-consuming.
Off-policy RL's stochastic exploration methods pose significant safety risks for inherently unstable humanoid robots during real-world data collection.
Existing approaches struggle to bridge robust large-scale policy pretraining in simulation with safe and data-efficient finetuning on physical humanoids.
results: LIFT's SAC pretraining achieves fast wall-clock convergence and enables robust zero-shot deployment of policies to physical humanoids like Booster T1, demonstrating stable locomotion on unseen real-world surfaces.

The framework achieves superior sim-to-sim finetuning, adapting to new velocity targets within approximately 4x10^4 environment steps (800 seconds of interaction) while significantly outperforming other RL baselines.
Real-world finetuning on the Booster T1 robot improves gait patterns and velocity tracking with only 80-590 seconds of collected data, demonstrating practical and data-efficient adaptation.
solution: Utilizes a three-stage pipeline: large-scale Soft Actor-Critic (SAC) policy pretraining in parallel simulation, offline physics-informed world model pretraining, and iterative finetuning.
Employs a hybrid world model that combines known Lagrangian dynamics with a learned neural network residual, trained on logged pretraining data.
Confines stochastic exploration for policy improvement entirely to rollouts within the learned world model during finetuning, using only deterministic actions in the real environment for safety.
paper_summary.summary: The LIFT framework addresses challenges in humanoid robot control by integrating large-scale SAC pretraining with a physics-informed world model, enabling robust zero-shot sim-to-real transfer and safe, data-efficient finetuning on physical robots within minutes of interaction.

title: Towards Bridging the Gap between Large-Scale Pretraining and Efficient Finetuning for Humanoid Control

abstract:
Training reinforcement learning policies using environment interaction data
collected from varying policies or dynamics presents a fundamental challenge.
Existing works often overlook the distribution discrepancies induced by policy
or dynamics shifts, or rely on specialized algorithms with task priors, thus
often resulting in suboptimal policy performances and high learning variances.
In this paper, we identify a unified strategy for online RL policy learning
under diverse settings of policy and dynamics shifts: transition occupancy
matching. In light of this, we introduce a surrogate policy learning objective
by considering the transition occupancy discrepancies and then cast it into a
tractable min-max optimization problem through dual reformulation. Our method,
dubbed Occupancy-Matching Policy Optimization (OMPO), features a specialized
actor-critic structure equipped with a distribution discriminator and a
small-size local buffer. We conduct extensive experiments based on the OpenAI
Gym, Meta-World, and Panda Robots environments, encompassing policy shifts
under stationary and nonstationary dynamics, as well as domain adaption. The
results demonstrate that OMPO outperforms the specialized baselines from
different categories in all settings. We also find that OMPO exhibits
particularly strong performance when combined with domain randomization,
highlighting its potential in RL-based robotics applications

organization_info.name: Institute for AI Industry Research, Tsinghua University

title: OMPO: A Unified Framework for RL under Policy and Dynamics Shifts

abstract: Existing reinforcement learning (RL) methods struggle with long-horizon robotic manipulation tasks, particularly those involving sparse rewards. While action chunking is a promising paradigm for robotic manipulation, using RL to directly learn continuous action chunks in a stable and data-efficient manner remains a critical challenge. This paper introduces AC3 (Actor-Critic for Continuous Chunks), a novel RL framework that learns to generate high-dimensional, continuous action sequences. To make this learning process stable and data-efficient, AC3 incorporates targeted stabilization mechanisms for both the actor and the critic. First, to ensure reliable policy improvement, the actor is trained with an asymmetric update rule, learning exclusively from successful trajectories. Second, to enable effective value learning despite sparse rewards, the critic's update is stabilized using intra-chunk $n$-step returns and further enriched by a self-supervised module providing intrinsic rewards at anchor points aligned with each action chunk. We conducted extensive experiments on 25 tasks from the BiGym and RLBench benchmarks. Results show that by using only a few demonstrations and a simple model architecture, AC3 achieves superior success rates on most tasks, validating its effective design.
title: Actor-Critic for Continuous Action Chunks: A Reinforcement Learning Framework for Long-Horizon Robotic Manipulation with Sparse Reward

abstract:
Despite extreme sample inefficiency, on-policy reinforcement learning, aka
policy gradients, has become a fundamental tool in decision-making problems.
With the recent advances in GPU-driven simulation, the ability to collect large
amounts of data for RL training has scaled exponentially. However, we show that
current RL methods, e.g. PPO, fail to ingest the benefit of parallelized
environments beyond a certain point and their performance saturates. To address
this, we propose a new on-policy RL algorithm that can effectively leverage
large-scale environments by splitting them into chunks and fusing them back
together via importance sampling. Our algorithm, termed SAPG, shows
significantly higher performance across a variety of challenging environments
where vanilla PPO and other strong baselines fail to achieve high performance.
Website at https://sapg-rl.github.io/
title: SAPG: Split and Aggregate Policy Gradients

abstract:
Reinforcement Learning (RL) has made significant strides in complex tasks but
struggles in multi-task settings with different embodiments. World model
methods offer scalability by learning a simulation of the environment but often
rely on inefficient gradient-free optimization methods for policy extraction.
In contrast, gradient-based methods exhibit lower variance but fail to handle
discontinuities. Our work reveals that well-regularized world models can
generate smoother optimization landscapes than the actual dynamics,
facilitating more effective first-order optimization. We introduce Policy
learning with multi-task World Models (PWM), a novel model-based RL algorithm
for continuous control. Initially, the world model is pre-trained on offline
data, and then policies are extracted from it using first-order optimization in
less than 10 minutes per task. PWM effectively solves tasks with up to 152
action dimensions and outperforms methods that use ground-truth dynamics.
Additionally, PWM scales to an 80-task setting, achieving up to 27% higher
rewards than existing baselines without relying on costly online planning.
Visualizations and code are available at this https URL

keyInsights: Well-regularized, learned world models can generate smoother optimization landscapes compared to the true, discontinuous dynamics of complex environments.
For first-order gradient (FoG) policy optimization, the smoothness of the world model's induced landscape is more critical than its absolute predictive accuracy.
Learned world models can provide more effective and robust gradients for policy learning than direct differentiation through ground-truth, stiff physics simulations.
originalProblem: Existing world-model-based RL methods rely on zeroth-order gradients (ZoG), leading to high variance and inefficient online planning for multi-task scalability.
First-order gradient (FoG) methods, while efficient, struggle with discontinuities (e.g., contact) inherent in real-world physics or ground-truth simulators.
Scaling traditional model-free reinforcement learning to diverse multi-task settings often results in sample inefficiency and poor generalization.
results: PWM achieved up to 27% higher rewards than TD-MPC2 in multi-task settings, extracting expert policies for new tasks in under 10 minutes (9.3 minutes on an Nvidia RTX6000 GPU).
Ablations showed that increased world model smoothness (e.g., via SimNorm) led to higher asymptotic policy rewards, despite slightly reduced world model accuracy.
PWM consistently outperformed methods using ground-truth differentiable dynamics (e.g., SHAC) in high-dimensional tasks and remained robust to increased contact stiffness.
solution: A multi-task world model, based on TD-MPC2's architecture and incorporating SimNorm activation for smoothness, is pre-trained offline.
Individual policies for specific tasks are then efficiently extracted by performing first-order gradient (FoG) optimization directly through the pre-trained world model.
Policy learning is decoupled from world model training, allowing batched, parallel imagined trajectories for rapid (under 10 minutes per task) policy acquisition.
paper_summary.summary: PWM proposes a framework for multi-task reinforcement learning that pre-trains a regularized world model offline to enable efficient first-order gradient policy optimization. This method allows for the extraction of expert policies for new tasks in under 10 minutes, outperforming prior approaches like TD-MPC2 by up to 27% in multi-task settings.

title: PWM: Policy Learning with Multi-Task World Models

abstract:
Reinforcement learning (RL) has shown great potential in training agile and
adaptable controllers for legged robots, enabling them to learn complex
locomotion behaviors directly from experience. However, policies trained in
simulation often fail to transfer to real-world robots due to unrealistic
assumptions such as infinite actuator bandwidth and the absence of torque
limits. These conditions allow policies to rely on abrupt, high-frequency
torque changes, which are infeasible for real actuators with finite bandwidth.
Traditional methods address this issue by penalizing aggressive motions
through regularization rewards, such as joint velocities, accelerations, and
energy consumption, but they require extensive hyperparameter tuning.
Alternatively, Lipschitz-Constrained Policies (LCP) enforce finite bandwidth
action control by penalizing policy gradients, but their reliance on gradient
calculations introduces significant GPU memory overhead. To overcome this
limitation, this work proposes Spectral Normalization (SN) as an efficient
replacement for enforcing Lipschitz continuity. By constraining the spectral
norm of network weights, SN effectively limits high-frequency policy
fluctuations while significantly reducing GPU memory usage. Experimental
evaluations in both simulation and real-world humanoid robot show that SN
achieves performance comparable to gradient penalty methods while enabling more
efficient parallel training.

keyInsights: Spectral Normalization (SN) offers a computationally efficient alternative to gradient penalty methods for enforcing Lipschitz continuity in policy networks.
By ensuring a 1-Lipschitz continuous policy network, SN inherently promotes smooth control commands, which are critical for real-world robotic systems with finite actuator bandwidth.
A tunable scaling coefficient for SN allows balancing policy smoothness with task performance and learning convergence.
originalProblem: Reinforcement learning policies trained in simulation often produce high-frequency, abrupt control commands that real-world robot actuators cannot execute, leading to instability and failure (sim-to-real gap).

Traditional methods like regularization rewards require extensive hyperparameter tuning, while Gradient Penalty-based Lipschitz-Constrained Policies (GP-LCP) consume excessive GPU memory, limiting parallel training environments.
The inability to efficiently generate smooth policies hinders the practical deployment of complex locomotion behaviors learned in simulation to physical robots.
results: The Spectral Normalization approach reduced GPU memory consumption to approximately 47% of available memory, similar to a baseline, significantly less than GP-LCP's 74% usage.

In simulation, SN achieved comparable control quality to GP-LCP, with significantly lower joint velocity, acceleration, and torque difference metrics compared to baseline policies (e.g., torque difference 9.25 for SN vs. 27.36 for baseline).
When deployed on the physical TOCABI humanoid robot, SN policies enabled stable walking across various command inputs, outperforming baseline policies which consistently failed due to vibrations.
solution: The authors propose applying Spectral Normalization (SN) to policy networks as an efficient method to constrain the Lipschitz constant of the neural network.
SN normalizes each weight matrix by its spectral norm (largest singular value), ensuring that each layer and thus the overall network is 1-Lipschitz continuous, promoting smooth control.
A scaling coefficient is introduced for the final layer's weights to provide flexibility in tuning the effective Lipschitz constant, balancing smoothness and task performance.
paper_summary.summary: Researchers at Seoul National University developed a computationally efficient method using Spectral Normalization for training reinforcement learning policies, enabling smoother, more stable control for humanoid robots in real-world deployment by significantly reducing GPU memory consumption compared to gradient penalty methods. The approach produced stable walking on a physical humanoid robot while requiring only 47% of GPU memory compared to 74% for previous Lipschitz-constrained policies.

title: Spectral Normalization for Lipschitz-Constrained Policies on Learning  Humanoid Locomotion

abstract: Massively parallel GPU simulation environments have accelerated reinforcement learning (RL) research by enabling fast data collection for on-policy RL algorithms like Proximal Policy Optimization (PPO). To maximize throughput, it is common to use short rollouts per policy update, increasing the update-to-data (UTD) ra- tio. However, we find that, in this setting, standard synchronous resets introduce harmful nonstationarity, skewing the learning signal and destabilizing training. We introduce staggered resets, a simple yet effective technique where environments are initialized and reset at varied points within the task horizon. This yields training batches with greater temporal diversity, reducing the nonstationarity induced by synchronized rollouts. We characterize dimensions along which RL environments can benefit significantly from staggered resets through illustrative toy environ- ments. We then apply this technique to challenging high-dimensional robotics environments, achieving significantly higher sample efficiency, faster wall-clock convergence, and stronger final performance. Finally, this technique scales better with more parallel environments compared to naive synchronized rollouts.

keyInsights: Massively parallel on-policy RL with short rollouts and synchronous resets inherently creates a cyclical nonstationarity in training data, distinct from general nonstationarity, which severely impacts learning.
This cyclical nonstationarity significantly degrades learning stability, sample efficiency, and final performance, particularly in tasks with long horizons or sequential skill dependencies.

A simple, computationally inexpensive, and algorithm-agnostic staggering of environment resets effectively mitigates this issue by ensuring temporal diversity in training batches, leading to more robust and scalable learning.
originalProblem:
Massively parallel on-policy reinforcement learning, especially with short rollouts (K 
 H) in GPU-accelerated simulators, suffers from cyclical nonstationarity in training data.
Synchronous environment resets cause training batches to be temporally homogeneous, exposing the learner to a constantly shifting, biased data stream from only narrow segments of the task.
This cyclical bias leads to unstable training, including value function divergence, policy oscillations, and catastrophic forgetting, undermining the benefits of high-throughput data generation.
results: Staggered resets substantially mitigate value function estimation errors (reducing MSE from over 80 to under 2.5 in toy environments), policy oscillations, and catastrophic forgetting (14x improved knowledge retention).

On high-dimensional robotics tasks (ManiSkill3, AllegroKuka), staggered resets consistently achieved faster wall-clock convergence, higher final success rates, and greater training stability (lower variance across seeds).

The method significantly improved scalability, allowing wall-clock convergence time to continue decreasing with increased parallelism (up to 6000+ environments), unlike naive synchronous setups which showed diminishing returns.
solution: The paper introduces "staggered resets," an algorithm-agnostic technique that desynchronizes the effective starting points of parallel environments across the task horizon.

Before training, each parallel environment is initialized with an `offset_steps_i`, positioning environments at diverse points in the task by executing random actions or an untrained policy for these offset steps.

During training, standard K-step rollouts proceed, but the aggregated N 
 K batch now contains a temporally heterogeneous mix of experiences from various task segments, providing a more stationary and representative data stream for policy updates.
paper_summary.summary: This research addresses cyclical nonstationarity in massively parallel on-policy reinforcement learning caused by synchronous environment resets and short rollouts.
